text: Rex just announced that they're going all in on Agentec Commerce. So today I'm here in their headquarters in downtown San Francisco to talk with their founder, Pedro, about why. Agentec Commerce is probably one of the most interesting subjects in fintech right now because hundreds of billions, if not trillions, of payments volume is up for grabs. There's 1.8 trillion of corporate cards penned in the U.S. today. Today we're going to talk about what is Agentec Commerce, why it matters, and why some of the most interesting opportunities are in B2B and not just consumer. When you go into B2B side, there's a lot more exciting stuff to build there because the decision making is a lot more complex. We're also going to zoom out and talk a little bit about how if you're a founder already operating at scale and you need to build a new product or feature that is AI centric, how you can build that within an existing organization. Because that's what Petro has had to do. I don't think you change the company. I think you have to start new things. I think this is an incredibly exciting time to be in fintech. It used to be that you had the same products from the same five big banks, and they were crappy and nothing really changed. But now you have these tech-native companies like Brex who are operating at scale, who are going all in on AI. I think it's really hard to be at the forefront of the product building if you're not at the forefront of the technology and really understanding what's actually happening. And I'm especially excited to talk to Pedro because he is an amazing engineer. At age 13, he was the first Brazilian to jailbreak the iPhone. At age 15, he founded the stripe of Brazil, Pagar May. And at age 22, he founded Brex, which is one of the fastest growing YC companies of all time. Now at age 29, he's going 996, working in person in San Francisco to build the future of eugenic commerce at Brex. Well, thanks so much for coming on. Of course. Thanks for having me. First of all, congratulations. I think you're just back recently married and back on your honeymoon. Yes. Yes. Very happy about it. Thank you. Are you ready to lock in now? Age of AI, agentic commerce is coming? Yes, ready for it. What I would like to do first, I think, is just define what is agentic commerce, because everyone's excited about it. Yeah. Like, what does it mean? And then we can talk about some of the most exciting areas within that general landscape. So I think at a high level, it means when you think about the buying decision and the buying process, how many decisions exist inside of it. So you could imagine, as a consumer, you want to buy a new piece of clothes You have to decide okay which brand I want where is the store located what price what alternatives I have what the delivery time And agents turn out to be quite good at a lot of that stuff and doing a lot of the decision making for you. But for us, I think the really interesting part is when you go in the B2B side, there's a lot more exciting stuff to build there because the decision making is a lot more complex. So when you think about a buying decision inside a company, of course, you have tons of decisions about, okay, what is actually in policy? Most companies have a procurement policy. How do you compare prices? How do you understand terms? How do you understand the security implications of buying a vendor, compliance requirements, and so on and so on? And a big part for us as well is not just the buying decision, but everything that happens after the purchase. So all the accounting implications, How do you book that? Who needs to approve? Who needs to review? Right. So the way we think about agents and the buying process is how do you orchestrate this entire process to deliver the end user and interface that is, hey, I need to buy this piece of software or I need to buy, you know, this furniture for my my new office or whatever it is. And the entire process happens and orchestrates automatically in the back end. Yeah. I think you and I are both lying that people think of a general commerce and I think they default to consumer. Yeah. And that's interesting, but I think the B2B use case makes 10x more sense. 100%. Yeah, because I think that when you think about, a lot of the way we think about agents inside companies and finance teams is every persona will have an agent, right? And when you just think about the number of people that get involved in a purchasing process in a company, you typically have the employer, the manager that wants to make the purchase. Then you have someone in the finance team that holds the budget. Then you have a procurement specialist that can typically handle the negotiation. Then you have, of course, legal. You have IT and security. You have a line of business. You have a line of business. You have, there's accounting implications of that. How do you treat that? You have, you know, all of these different, you have maybe some sort of integration you have to do. So all these different components are factored into the decision. And we think that each one and one of them will have agents that will help orchestrate the decision making that you have to make that purchase. Yeah, so in consumer land, it's like you decide to buy something, you hit a button, it shows up the next day. Exactly. B2B land? Not that simple. You like. Not that simple. It might take six months. Exactly And even like a simple purchase right One of the things we talk a lot about is even if you go swipe your card to go on a trip right There a lot more to it than the swipe because you have to get a receipt for that get a memo fill out all the attendees deal with the accounting any approvals if you have a reimbursement because you had an out expense All these things require so much manner of work, and we see a world where agents will coordinate more of that work automatically to a point that it's just literally describing your thoughts and a lot of the work happening for you. Yeah. I think every founder right now is going through the phase of, like, AI has happened. I need to figure out what to do with my business in this new era. I'm curious when you kind of had that initial realization, like, I need to do something here, and I want to focus on agentic commerce. Yeah, so for us, I think there's like, the way we thought about AI in general is there's really two big avenues to work on. One is what we call product AI. So how do we rethink our product? And really the prompt for us there was like, if we started the company again in 2025 versus 2017, how would we build Brex? And it turns out a lot of things would be different. And what we decided to do is say, let's just go and build that version of Brex ourselves because otherwise someone else, a new startup, a new cover, we're going to go and do. So how do you disrupt yourself there? And really the interesting thing there is the mindset and the way you do it and the way you structure teams. And there's a lot of things that are different. The other part of AI is like all the what we call operational AI, which is how do you operate as a company much more efficiently because of AI. And for us, there's really two use cases that are really interesting. One is operations. Like we have a lot of people that are just literally managing, you know, large amounts of money movement, support, customer issues, implementation, like all the things that deal with the operations of Brex because we move, you know, billions and billions of dollars a month. And then you have, you know, at the same time, go-to-market teams that are, you know, going after customers and growing and, you know, generating demand. And there's a lot of use cases there. And I think the common thread across both is that I don't think you change the company. I think you have to start new things, right? So I think the discontinuities in AI, you know, And I think when you think about the processes that you have, they are designed not to change. They're designed to effectively be relatively incremental from where things were in the past. So the way we done it is actually saying you know let have separate new units that can rethink some of these processes end to end with technology we have today both on the product side and the operation side And on the product side, you know, for example, we were actually bringing to market a pretty big product release in a couple of months, actually weeks now, that basically, you know, shifts a lot of the way customers interact with Rex. and the way we've built it was effectively, you know, zero basing a lot of the sort of end user experience and thinking how would we build with agents. And I think the kinds of people and the kinds of ways you run the team and the culture has to look a lot more like a 10-person, 20-person startup than an 1,100-person company that is Rex today. So a lot of it is replicating these like zero-to-one environments in a much larger company to build that ethos and momentum and velocity again. Yeah. And a lot of the work you talked about for AI, like it's process optimization internally. And that's hard, but doable and aligns with your existing business model. But then there's also the scope of work, which is like maybe we have to be willing to disrupt ourselves. And so I'm curious to talk about that, how you approach that kind of thought with regards to authentic commerce and what you're thinking about building or are building in this. I think the best way to think about it is when the company starts to have multiple lines of products and you have multiple different customers using you, you start to sort of have this mode where the company starts to move in a direction that becomes the demands of customers with the requirements they have today. Yeah. So I think the reason companies move relatively incrementally is because customers are pulling the company away from you. They start pulling certain features, certain integrations, certain requirements, certain products. And I think a lot of the way we thought about it is like, you know, a big topic, of course, in AI is like automation, right? Like how do you just replace a lot of labor that finance teams have to do manually? And three years ago, a big thing that we did is we said, hey, most finding teams thought of expense systems as a way of reaching compliance. And we used to pound the table and say, hey, no, actually, you have to buy automation because automation's far more impactful in a process that touches every employee in your company. And now we're seeing for the first time, you know, RFPs coming in saying, oh, no, we actually care a lot
